RNA information: Bacillus endophyticus 107yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, and its structural characteristics and functional mechanisms show significant diversity and specificity in regulating gene expression. It is composed of 107 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures: P1, P2, P3 and P4, among which the P1 and P2 stem ring structures play a key role in ligand binding.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.

RNA information: B4-25 is a 100-nucleotide RNA aptamer isolated from a random-sequence pool by Ellington & Szostak through in vitro selection against Reactive Blue 4; it contains a 28-nt highly conserved core that folds into a stem–loop–stem motif predicted by GCG-FOLD, presenting a pre-organized pocket whose planar, negatively charged surface complements the anthraquinone and sulfonate groups of the dye, achieving ~600 fM affinity and >50 % column retention with virtually no cross-binding to other dyes.

Small_molecule_information: 2-phenylacetic acid, also referred to as Phenylacetic acid, is a simple aromatic compound and an aromatic fatty acid with the formula C8H8O2, its structure is characterized by a benzene ring attached to an acetyl group and features a benzene ring attached to an acetic acid group (a methylene CH2 group linked to a carboxyl COOH group); this compound has a dissociation constant that does not reach the level of an active ligand and does not induce gene expression, suggesting that it may not be an active ligand for 108yjdF RNA, it occurs naturally in certain plants and essential oils and is a product of tryptophan metabolism in mammals, in real-world applications, it possesses a strong, honey-like odor, making it valuable as a fragrance and flavoring agent, particularly in the manufacture of certain floral perfumes and food essences, it is also an important precursor in the synthesis of various compounds, notably being used as a side-chain precursor in the production of Penicillin G, and in medicine, its derivatives are utilized to treat certain forms of hyperammonemia caused by urea cycle disorders, by assisting in the clearance of excess nitrogen from the body.

Small_molecule_information: 4,5-dioxo-1H-pyrrolo[2,3-f]quinoline-2,7,9-tricarboxylic acid, also referred to as Pyrroloquinoline quinone (PQQ), is a complex polycyclic compound with the formula C14H6N2O8, and it is also a planar azaaromatic redox cofactor structured with a tricyclic ring system (fused pyrrole, quinoline, and quinone rings) which is based on the pyrrolo[2,3-f]quinoline framework, featuring two ketone groups at positions 4 and 5, three carboxylic acid groups at positions 2, 7, and 9these carboxylic acid groups are key for its electron transfer ability. This highly functionalized, multi-ring structure provides multiple sites for metal chelation and potential biological interaction, and it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis. Structurally, it bears resemblance to the cores of biologically active natural products, such as certain quinones and tricarboxylic acid derivatives; its core role is supporting bacterial enzyme function as it acts as a coenzyme for dehydrogenases, facilitating redox reactions in metabolism, and it is also studied for potential antioxidant effects in eukaryotes. In real-world research, compounds with such skeletons are typically explored as potential tyrosine kinase inhibitors or DNA topoisomerase inhibitors, targets that are crucial in the development of anticancer drugs. Furthermore, its numerous oxygen and nitrogen atoms suggest it could act as an effective metal ion chelator, potentially applicable in environmental or pharmaceutical contexts, though its specific application requires further dedicated study.

Small_molecule_information: Benzoic acid is the simplest aromatic carboxylic acid with the formula C7H6O2. Its structure consists of a benzene ring directly attached to a single carboxyl group (COOH), which is the key structural feature of this simple aromatic carboxylic acid. Benzoic acid occurs naturally in many plants, such as gum benzoin and berries. The most significant real-world application of Benzoic acid is its use as a food preservative, commonly added to food, beverages, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals. It primarily functions by inhibiting the growth of microorganisms, especially yeasts and molds, through the reduction of intracellular pH and interference with cellular enzyme systems. Its sodium salt, Sodium benzoate, is often preferred for its better water solubility. Additionally, Benzoic acid is an important precursor for the synthesis of other organic chemicals, including benzoyl chloride and benzoate esters. Relevant data shows that benzoic acid does not have a low Kd value and does not induce gene expression, indicating that it may not be an active ligand for 108yjdF RNA.

Small_molecule_information: Pyridine-2,3-dicarboxylic acid, also referred to as Quinolinic acid (QA), is a planar azaaromatic dicarboxylic acid with the formula C7H5NO4, featuring a quinoline ring (fused benzene and pyridine rings) that bears carboxylic acid groups (-COOH) at the 2- and 3-positions; it is a dicarboxylic acid derivative of pyridine and an important endogenous metabolite as well as an intermediate in the kynurenine pathway of tryptophan-nicotinic acid metabolism, generated in both the brain and peripheral tissues of mammals, and it should be noted that QA binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while it also serves as a key intermediate in the biosynthesis of NAD (a critical coenzyme); in biomedical research, QAs activity is primarily linked to its neurotoxicity: it acts as an excitotoxic amino acid, functioning as an agonist for the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor, which can lead to overstimulation and death of neurons at high concentrations, therefore, elevated levels of QA in the blood and cerebrospinal fluid are considered a potential contributing factor and marker for neuroinflammatory conditions and several neurodegenerative diseases like Huntingtons disease and Alzheimers disease.

Small_molecule_information: 1-[(2R,3R,4S,5R)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-(phosphonooxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]pyridin-1-ium-3-carboxylate is a zwitterionic form of Nicotinic Acid Mononucleotide (NaMN), also referred to as Nicotinic acid mononucleotide (NAMN), which is a precursor to Nicotinamide Mononucleotide (NMN), with the formula C11H14NO10P, it is a non-azaaromatic nucleotide that lacks planar azaaromatic features and binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, its core structure is composed of a nicotinic acid moiety (pyridine ring attached to the sugar at N1 and a carboxylate at 3) linked to a ribose phosphate group (a D-ribose ring with a phosphate group at the 5 position, which is consistent with the structure of a nicotinic acid base, a ribose sugar, and one phosphate group linked to the riboses 5-carbon), this molecule is a vital metabolic intermediate found in all living cells, playing a critical role in cellular energy metabolism, functionally, NaMN (also NAMN) is a key intermediate in NAD biosynthesis as well as a step in the synthesis of Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide (NAD), cells convert it to nicotinic acid adenine dinucleotide (NAAD) first, then to NAD, supporting essential redox reactions, and NAD is a necessary coenzyme for hundreds of enzymatic reactions crucial for cell health, energy production, and DNA repair, in recent years, molecules of this class have gained significant attention for their potential in anti-aging research, as they are considered potential agents for boosting cellular NAD levels, improving metabolic function, and potentially slowing down the aging process.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(1H-indol-3-yl)ethanamine, also referred to as Tryptamine, is a planar azaaromatic biogenic amine consisting of an indole ring (a fused benzene and pyrrole ring) covalently linked to an ethylamine side chain (-CH-CH-NH), and it is a fundamental biological molecule that is the decarboxylated product of the amino acid Tryptophan; it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and it is a neurotransmitter and neuromodulator found in the central nervous system and the gastrointestinal tract, with its core role being as a biological precursor: it is the building block for neurotransmitters (e.g., serotonin) and alkaloids (e.g., melatonin), also acts as a signaling molecule in plants and microorganisms, and serves as the biochemical precursor for many more complex psychoactive and regulatory molecules, including serotonin, melatonin, and psilocybin; in the real world, Tryptamine and its derivatives are key focuses of neuroscience research due to their influence on mood, sleep, and cognitive function, forming the structural basis for numerous psychedelic and psychoactive drugs (e.g., DMT).

Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

Small_molecule_information: 4-aminobenzoic acid is commonly known as p-Aminobenzoic acid (PABA), an amino acid and carboxylic acid derivative, structured as a benzoic acid molecule with an amino group at position 4 of the benzene ring, molecular formula C7H7NO2, and 4-Aminobenzoic acid is an aromatic amino acid characterized by an amino group and a carboxyl group attached to a benzene ring, in terms of real-world activity, PABA plays a crucial role as a biochemical precursor in microorganisms and plants, it is an essential intermediate required by bacteria for the synthesis of folic acid, sulfonamide drugs (e.g., sulfamethoxazole) exert their potent antibacterial effect by structurally mimicking PABA and competitively inhibiting the key enzyme (dihydropteroate synthase) in the bacterial folic acid synthesis pathway, for humans, PABA is a non-essential nutrient, but its derivatives were once widely used in sunscreens for their effective UVB absorption properties, the figure indicates that 4-aminobenzoic acid has weak binding affinity and does not induce gene expression, suggesting that it may not be an active ligand for 108yjdF RNA.

RNA information: The miR-96 precursor( pri-miR-96) is a structured RNA hairpin that is specifically recognized by a benzimidazole small molecule (compound 1) identified via the Inforna pipeline. Binding occurs at the single-stranded Drosha-processing site within the precursor, thereby blocking its cleavage and reducing mature miR-96 production. This selective interaction relieves miR-96-mediated repression of the tumor-suppressor FOXO1 mRNA, restoring cellular FMN/riboflavin-like homeostasis by rebalancing oncogenic miRNA levels and apoptotic control in cancer cells.; The pri-miR-96 transcript features a critical hairpin structure containing a Drosha processing site. Its key functional element is a UU internal loop and a nearby GG internal loop within this hairpin, which serve as the binding site for the small molecule Targaprimir-96. Binding of the molecule to this specific structural pocket inhibits Drosha cleavage, thereby blocking the biogenesis of mature oncogenic miR-96. This inhibition leads to the upregulation of the FOXO1 protein and induces apoptosis in cancer cells, highlighting the direct link between its specific secondary structure and its oncogenic function.

also referred to as Compound 2, is a hybrid molecule of extreme structural complexity, representing the pinnacle of multi-functional drug design, and Compound 2 is a chimeric molecule composed of the RNA-binding small molecule Targapremir-96 (Compound 1) covalently conjugated to the cytotoxic natural product bleomycin A5, its structure incorporates at least three main functional domains: first, a glycosyl or nucleotide derivative part (with multiple oxan rings and hydroxy groups), likely to enhance cell permeability or target specific membrane receptors, second, a benzimidazole-DNA targeting affecting healthy breast epithelial cells (MCF-10A), while its DNA cleavage activity and associated DNA damage are significantly attenuated compared to the control Compound 3.

Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-6-amine, also referred to as Adenine (a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound), is one of the four fundamental nucleobases that constitute nucleic acids (DNA and RNA), structured as a purine ring with an amino group at position 6, molecular formula C5H5N5. In terms of real-world activity, Adenine is a vital building block for life; it is an essential component of genetic material, pairing with Thymine (T) in the DNA double helix and Uracil (U) in RNA via hydrogen bonds, forming the basis for storing and expressing genetic information, is the core structure of the cells energy currency ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate), ADP, and AMP, participating in all major cellular energy transfer processes, and is a constituent of several important coenzymes (e.g., NAD, FAD), involved in a wide range of redox reactions and metabolic regulation; additionally, Adenine is embedded in the core of the pbuE ribose switch with a simple 6-aminopine plane, inducing local base rearrangement and stabilizing the P1 helix, rapidly occupies RNA within the instantaneous window of transcription, thereby inhibiting terminon folding and enabling the expression of downstream efference pump genes, and serves as a natural start button for bacteria to sense and excrete excess purine and maintain intracellular homeostasis.

Small_molecule_information: 3,7-dihydropurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as Xanthine (systematically 9H-xanthine or 2,6-dioxopurine), is a naturally occurring purine base and oxopurine bearing carbonyls at C-2 and C-6 and a proton on N-9, with the formula C5H4N4O2. Its structure is a purine ring with keto groups at positions 2 and 6, and it exists in tautomeric equilibrium with its 7H form as well as tautomeric forms with NH bonds at positions 3 and 7. Biologically, Xanthine itself is generally inactive, but it is the parent scaffold for many important alkaloids such as caffeine, theophylline, and theobromine, which are its methylated derivatives; notably, because it lacks the N-3 methyl and the N-7 hydrogen bond donor present in theophylline, xanthine binds the TCT8-4 RNA only weakly, illustrating how the RNAs hydrophobic pocket and hydrogen-bond network have been evolved to discriminate sub-angstrom differences within the xanthine scaffold. In clinical medicine, drugs like Allopurinol are used to treat gout and hyperuricemia by inhibiting xanthine oxidase, thereby reducing the conversion of xanthine to uric acid.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]ethanone, also referred to as Compound 17 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative modified on the piperazines secondary amine purchased from Asinex that binds the influenza A RNA promoter and has improved anti-influenza activity), has a simple acetyl group as its acyl side chain; acetylation is a common simplification strategy and serves as a baseline for evaluating the contribution of more complex side chains, and in terms of real-world activity, the core remains the 1-adrenergic receptor antagonist pharmacophore, while its simplicity suggests it might have good bioavailability, or function as a prodrug that is hydrolyzed in vivo to the more active unsubstituted piperazine derivative (6,7-dimethoxy-2-piperazin-1-ylquinazolin-4-amine), potentially offering a sustained or targeted release profile.

Small_molecule_information: 5-amino-1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]pentan-1-one, also referred to as Compound 4 (a derivative of DPQ/Compound 1 with modifications on the secondary amine of its piperazine ring), is a compound featuring a quinazoline-piperazine scaffold, where the quinazoline moiety is the typical -adrenoceptor antagonist pharmacophore (4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazoline). At the other nitrogen of the piperazine ring, a flexible 5-aminopentanoyl side chain is attached. The presence of the terminal primary amine (NH) makes the compound more polar and hydrophilic, and potentially protonated at physiological pH. This design is often used to modulate the drugs water solubility, pharmacokinetics, or enhance its interaction with polar binding sites. The compound is synthesized via specific steps, can bind to the influenza A virus RNA promoter, exhibits no significant anti-influenza activity and no obvious cytotoxicity, and in practice, it could be researched as a next-generation -adrenoceptor antagonist, with the amino side chain possibly aiming to improve the drugs bioavailability or reduce side effects.

RNA information: RNA5 is a 29-nt synthetic hairpin RNA based on SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5, containing a single G bulge. It spontaneously folds into an A-form helix with a flexible bulge site, enabling minor-groove recognition. RNA5 is used for GaMD simulations, NMR R2 mapping, and Kd measurements to probe selective ligand binding.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-1-yl]-1-[(E)-prop-1-enyl]quinolin-2-one;N-methylmethanimine;prop-1-ene, also referred to as SMSM64, is a complex polycyclic heterocycle based on a quinolin-2-one core that replaces the coumarin core of C30 with a bulkier N-pyridinyl-quinolone, destroying planarity and hydrogen-bond geometry and consequently failing to enter the RNA minor groove, validating core rigidity and polarity as essential for G-bulge binding. It is substituted at the 1-position with a trans-propenyl group, at the 3-position with an imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine fused bicyclic system, and at the 7-position with a chiral 3-methylpiperazinyl moiety. Both the quinolinone and imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine frameworks are common pharmacophores in drugs with Central Nervous System (CNS) activity, such as those for anxiety, insomnia, or psychosis. The piperazine substituent often improves water solubility and bioavailability and acts as a key binding site. This molecules precise stereochemistry and polycyclic nature suggest it is a highly refined candidate drug targeting neurotransmitter receptors, aimed at treating psychiatric or neurological disorders, with the other two components in the name being residues or solvents.

Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-2-methoxypropan-1-one, also referred to as Compound 11 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative with modifications on the piperazines secondary amine where R1-methoxyethyl and synthesized via one-step coupling), is similar to 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-2-methylpropan-1-one, the core activity of this compound is predicted to be 1-adrenergic receptor antagonism, and it binds the influenza A RNA promoter similarly to compound 1 (causing U27 imino proton line broadening) yet exhibits no significant anti-influenza activity in cell-based assays; its side chain, 2-methoxypropanoyl, introduces a methoxy group to increase local polarity, which can affect the molecules aqueous solubility and biological membrane permeability, and in practice, this polarity adjustment is used to optimize the compounds pharmacokinetic (PK) profile and metabolic pathways, potentially resulting in a different half-life or excretion pattern for treating specific patient populations with hypertension or BPH.

Small_molecule_information: 3-(7-fluoroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl)-7-piperazin-4-ium-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C30, is a mono-cationic coumarin derivative combining the coumarin scaffold with a 7-fluoro-substituted imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine, with the molecular formula C20H18FN4O2, and it is a non-planar coumarin analog with a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system that carries a 1 charge (the positive charge is on the piperazin-4-ium group at position 7, and a fluoro atom is at position 7 of the imidazopyridine ring). Functionally, STPY-xpt can specifically recognize and bind to guanine. This binding event triggers the structural rearrangement of the riboswitch, thereby regulating the expression of downstream genes. Specifically, after binding to guanine, STPY-xpt tends to form a terminating substructure, leading to premature transcription termination. Furthermore, STPY-xpt demonstrated a relatively high 2AP (2-aminopine) binding affinity in the experiment, which is consistent with its highly efficient transcriptional regulatory efficiency in vivo. This high affinity may enable STPY-xpt to effectively regulate gene expression even at a lower concentration of guanine, thereby playing a key role in the metabolic regulation of bacteria.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: LL-xpt is a guanine riboswitch from Lactococcus lactis. Its structural feature is manifested as a specific RNA secondary structure, which contains three stem-loop regions , and these regions are organized around a three-way junction. The binding site of guanine was formed. The function of LL-xpt is to specifically recognize and bind guanine through its aptamer region, thereby regulating the expression of downstream genes. When guanine binds to the aptamer region, it causes a rearrangement of RNA structure, which may lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of related genes. In the experiment, LL-xpt demonstrated a binding affinity for 2-aminopine (2AP), which can serve as an alternative indicator for studying its binding ability to guanine. This difference in binding affinity may reflect the response ability of different ribose switches to changes in ligand concentration in vivo, thereby playing an important role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria.

RNA information: BS-xpt is a guanine riboswitch from Bacillus subtilis. Its aptamer domain centers on a three-way junction (helices P1, P2, P3) forming the ligand-binding site, with a conserved core and variable positions 24, 48, 73. It negatively modulates transcription upon guanine binding.

RNA information: OI-guaA refers to a guanine riboswitch from Oceanobacillus iheyensis. The structural features of this ribose switch include a specific RNA secondary structure, which is usually composed of three stem-ring regions (P1, P2 and P3), and these regions are organized around a three-way junction point, forming the guanine binding site. The function of OI-guaA is to specifically recognize and bind guanine through its aptamer region, thereby regulating gene expression. When guanine binds to the aptamer region, it triggers a rearrangement of RNA structure. This structural change may lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of downstream genes. This regulatory mechanism of the OI-guaA ribose switch enables it to respond rapidly when the intracellular guanine concentration changes, thereby playing a key role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria.

RNA information: Ca.LSU (Candida albicans Large Subunit) is a self-splicing intron of Class I located in the ribosome large subunit of the Candida albicans 26S rRNA gene. It plays a key role in the maturation process of rRNA, removing itself from the rRNA precursor through the self-splicing mechanism, thereby allowing for further processing and maturation of rRNA. The structure and function of Ca.LSU are of vital importance in the biosynthesis of ribosomes. Its correct folding and catalytic activity are crucial for the maturation of rRNA and the assembly of ribosomes. Studies have shown that certain compounds, such as pentamidine, can inhibit the self-splicing activity of Ca.LSU by binding to specific regions of introns and altering their folding structure. This inhibitory effect can be partially or completely reversed by adding positively charged molecules such as magnesium ions, calcium ions or arginine, which indicates that these positively charged molecules compete with pentamidine for binding sites, protecting the normal folding and function of introns.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)propoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Propamidine, is also a bis-amidine ether, but with a shorter propoxy ether linker chain (Propamidine molecules are relatively short and consist only of para-amidino-phenoxy groups connected by three methylene Bridges, presenting a short-arm diamidine configuration, whose length and flexibility limit the deep insertion within the RNA groove, allowing only loose binding to the peripheral asymmetric loop and lacking stable multivalent interactions, thus having almost no significant inhibition on the splicing activity of Class I introns), and similar to 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)pentoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, this class of bis-amidine ethers is designed as potential DNA-binding drugs with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, and due to the differing chain lengths, the molecules mode of DNA binding, cell membrane permeability, and selectivity against different pathogens may vary.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[(4-methylanilino)methyl]naphthalen-2-ol, also referred to as CP3, is a naphthol-containing compound whose structure is based on the naphthol parent skeleton: a benzene ring is fused with another benzene ring to form the naphthalene skeleton, which is substituted with a hydroxyl group at position 2 and a (4-methylanilino)methyl group at position 1, and this amphiphilic structure built around a naphthalene core with a hydroxyl group connected via a methylene bridge to an amino group and a 4-methylphenyl ring allowed moderate RNA binding, but it was not among the most potent compounds in biological assays; in terms of biological function, naphthalenes and their derivatives generally exhibit diverse biological activities such as antifungal, anti-inflammatory, or are used as fluorescent probes.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: methyl N-(4-chlorophenyl)-2H-quinoline-1-carboximidothioate, also referred to as CP2, is a quinoline-based molecule featuring a thione and imine functionality (which incorporates a quinoline core linked to a thiocarbamate group and a 4-chlorophenyl ring, with the combination of its planar aromatic system and halogen substituent contributing to its affinity for CAG repeat RNA and efficacy in reducing polyQ aggregation in cellular models, marking it as a promising candidate). Quinoline scaffolds are associated with various anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, and this complex structure is a candidate for research as an enzyme inhibitor, possibly targeting microbial enzymes, due to its specialized reactive groups.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6: This is a six-repeat control RNA with perfect Watson-Crick base pairing. It is used to evaluate the specificity of compound binding and shows minimal structural perturbation upon ligand interaction, confirming that small molecules do not significantly bind to regular duplex RNA.; The (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6 sequence serves as the extended control duplex. Its repetitive yet perfectly paired structure lacks the distinctive features of disease-repeat RNA, allowing researchers to conclusively prove that compound efficacy is due to specific recognition of the pathological mismatch motif and not general RNA binding.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.

Small_molecule_information: 3-methyl-2-pyridin-4-ylquinoline, also referred to as CP4, is a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound consisting of a naphthyridine fused heterocycle with methyl and pyridine substitutions; its structure uses the quinoline core, with a pyridin-4-yl substituent at the 2-position and a methyl group at the 3-position, the molecular formula is CHN, this compound contains two nitrogen heterocyclic rings and has an extensive conjugated system, in terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, and the introduction of the pyridine ring is often used to enhance coordination capability and modify biological activity.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[[1-[3-[[4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidoyl]amino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methyl]amino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-2-[[1-[3-[[4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidoyl]amino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]-N-propylacetamide, also referred to as 2AU-2 (a dimeric compound with two AU-azide modules spaced by two propylamine units), is a symmetrical bis-head molecule whose core active unit is an amidine and benzamidine derivative; this compound shows enhanced affinity, positive cooperativity, displaces bound proteins, reduces nuclear foci, mitochondrial dysfunction, and caspase-3 activity in SCA10 cells, and its amidine group is an important cationic functional group, the core active system of this molecule is a polyamidine-containing large conjugated planar system, exhibiting potent DNA minor groove binding ability, similar to classic anti-protozoal drugs like Pentamidine, the molecule links two amidine active units via a long chain containing triazole and polyamide segments, forming a bis-head structure designed to enhance DNA binding affinity and potentially achieve DNA cross-linking, therefore, the predicted real-world activity for this molecule is as a highly effective anti-protozoal, anti-fungal agent or a novel broad-spectrum antibiotic.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-[4-[N-(3-azidopropyl)carbamimidoyl]phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as AU-azide, is a highly functionalized probe molecule with the molecular formula C24H23N7O, and AU-azide is a derivative of Compound 2 with an azide group for conjugation; it exhibits even higher AU selectivity and is used to build multivalent compounds like dimers, its core is a planar diaryl-furan (biphenyl-furan) system, which provides hydrophobicity and - stacking ability, the molecule contains two key functional groups: carboximidamide and azido, the carboximidamide group (-C(NH)NH2) is typically positively charged at physiological pH, allowing it to interact strongly with negatively charged biomolecules like DNA; thus, amidine derivatives are often used as DNA minor groove binders with anti-tumor or antimicrobial activity, the azido group (-N3) is a prominent bio-orthogonal chemistry functional group, specifically a core component of Click Chemistry, which permits the molecule to be covalently linked to alkyne-containing partners under specific conditions, therefore, this compound is designed as a bifunctional molecular probe for target identification and bio-orthogonal chemical labeling.

RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 10, is an acridine-triazine dimer with acridine substituted at position 4, and the linker is a longer polyethylamino chain containing four ethylamino units (an oligoamino linker), molecular formula C60H67N21O2, and this potent dimeric ligand demonstrated strong binding and inhibition with a notable Tm, a much lower KD than the monomer, and a significant bivalent effect. In terms of real-world activity, its core function is as an ultra-potent DNA intercalator/cross-linker and topoisomerase inhibitor. Compared to 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, the increase in linker chain length means the molecule carries more positive charges, which generally further enhances its non-specific electrostatic affinity for DNA and may alter the span across the DNA double helix, affecting DNA cross-linking efficiency and topoisomerase toxicity. The synthesis and study of such compounds aim to systematically explore the influence of linker structure on the activity and pharmacokinetics of multi-target drugs, to develop more effective anticancer therapies.

RNA information: RNA1 contains the 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop motif that is present within the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96. It serves as the specific binding target for compound 1, with a Kd of 1,300 nM, but does not bind compound 2.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: RNA1 contains the 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop motif that is present within the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96. It serves as the specific binding target for compound 1, with a Kd of 1,300 nM, but does not bind compound 2.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a mono-benzimidazole Click Chemistry probe with tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module previously identified by the Informa platform to avidly bind a 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop within the Drosha processing site of the pri-miR-96 hairpin precursor, inhibits miR-96 biogenesis and induces apoptosis in breast cancer cells with activity that limits its in vivo utility, shows no detectable binding to DNA, and its real-world activity is dedicated to biological orthogonal labeling experiments for target validation and mechanism elucidation.

It features a single-nucleotide change compared to RNA1, which eliminates binding, confirming that the compounds' interactions with other RNAs are specific to the intended motifs.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-phenyl-N-pyridin-4-ylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 5, is a thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative, substituted with phenyl and pyridyl groups (with the phenyl substitution at the C-6 position of its pyridine ring), which shows no detectable binding to HIV-1 TAR RNA but binds HIV-1 RRE RNAa key element for viral replication; targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with RRE RNA, it has moderate affinity and lacks further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, and this class of compounds receives attention for its wide range of biological activities, including antineoplastic, antiviral, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory effects, with research suggesting that some derivatives can act as herbicide safeners or potent enzyme inhibitors.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-cyclopropyl-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 10, is an amide compound containing thienopyridine and cyclopropyl structures, modified from initial active analogs; its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a cyclopropylcarboxamide group at C2; in terms of biological function, Thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamides are a new class of antiplasmodial agents (anti-malarial) with strong antiparasitic activity, and this compound binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, exhibiting moderate affinity and lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-phenyl-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as compound 4, is a fused heterocyclic amide based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine scaffold, and it is also a 3-aminothieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative characterized by a phenyl substituent at the 6-position, a structural modification critical for its enhanced potency as a TAR RNA binder. This tricyclic backbone features an amino group at position 3 and a phenyl group at position 6. It is linked via an amide bond to a 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl substituent. The molecule is highly lipophilic, thanks to the phenyl group and the crucial trifluoromethyl (CF3) group. The trifluoromethyl group is a classic substituent in medicinal chemistry, used to enhance metabolic stability and transmembrane permeability, while also acting as a strong electron-withdrawing group. This planar, highly lipophilic amidic heterocycle is a prime example of a small molecule drug, often investigated as a non-competitive or competitive inhibitor of signal pathway enzymes like tyrosine kinases, with broad applicability in anti-cancer, immunomodulatory, and anti-inflammatory fields; additionally, it specifically interacts with key nucleobases in the HIV-1 TAR RNA elementG21, U23, and G43demonstrating dual binding affinity for both the bulge region (U23) and the lower stem (G43), a unique interaction profile that establishes it as a promising lead molecule for developing antiviral therapeutics that target RNA-protein interactions.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]ethyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-3NPr, is a polymeric peptidomimetic linked to an ethylenediamine backbone, containing three repeating D-alanine-amide units as the linker arm, and 2H-3NPr was one of the stronger members of the NPr series, which displayed effective disruption of the r(CUG)MBNL1 complex, though in cellular assays its activity was only moderate and did not match lysine-based scaffolds with better stability, demonstrating that an intermediate spacer length confers stronger in vitro potency but other chemical properties limited its therapeutic potential; this design of the polymeric peptidomimetic, featuring a reduced number of repeating units compared to the four-unit analogues, is specifically intended for the systematic study of the effect of linker length on the binding mode and affinity of the bis-benzimidazole molecule to DNA, and this medium-length linker is crucial for precisely optimizing the molecules coverage and spatial fit within the target site, such as the DNA minor groove, while as a highly cationic DNA binder, its potential real-world applications are focused on antitumor and antimicrobial drug development.

predicted real-world activity is as a potent anti-cancer or anti-fungal agent that disrupts nucleic acid function.

RNA information: The miR-210 precursor is a structured RNA hairpin whose transcription is switched on by the hypoxia-inducible factor HIF-1α binding to a conserved hypoxia-responsive element in its promoter. This “hypoxia riboswitch-like” mechanism maintains cellular homeostasis under low oxygen by rapidly up-regulating mature miR-210, which in turn represses iron-sulfur cluster assembly proteins and other metabolic targets, thereby tilting metabolism from oxidative phosphorylation to glycolysis and protecting cells from hypoxic damage .
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (Ht-N3, a bis-benzimidazole derivative with Hoechst 33258 core, 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethoxy side chain, azide group, and 4-fluorophenylethyl tail replacing FMNs ribose-phosphate), is an azide-containing bis-benzimidazole probe without the tert-butyl groups (compared to N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), its core activity remains the same: strong DNA binding, and it additionally binds 5CGG/3GGC and pre-miR-210, disrupts r(CGG)-DGCR8, restores SMN2/Bcl-x splicing, inhibits tumor growth, has low cytotoxicity while tRNA reduces its selectivity, serving as a probe/lead for FXTAS, CGG-repeat disorders, and miR-210-driven cancers; its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for nucleic acid binding studies and labeling, which enables researchers to map the interaction site of the bis-benzimidazole unit in a biological context using the high specificity of the azide group.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

RNA information: ATP-54-1 is an active RNA molecule with a length of 54 nucleotides, obtained by analyzing the 5' and 3' deletions of the ATP-binding RNA clone C8-ATP-3. Structurally, its core feature is an 11-nucleotide consensus sequence flanked by base-paired stems (the deletion of the left-hand stem directly abolishes its ATP-binding activity), and all clones containing this structure have an invariant unpaired G opposite the 11-nucleotide consensus sequence, which can fold into a specific secondary structure as a whole. Functionally, it binds ATP with high affinity, with a dissociation constant (Kd) of less than 50 μM, and a conformational change of RNA occurs when binding to ATP (confirmed by kethoxal modification experiments). It exhibits specificity for ATP binding—modification or removal of groups such as the 2' hydroxyl group on ATP and the 6-amino group of adenine significantly reduces binding affinity, and it also serves as an important basis for the subsequent design of the shorter 40-nucleotide ATP-binding RNA (ATP-40-1)
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3R,4S,5S)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-purin-9-yloxolan-2-yl]dihydrogenphosphate, also referred to as Adenosine Monophosphate (AMP), with the chemical structure C10H13N5O7P, is a typical nucleotide molecule. The compound is composed of a purine nucleobase (specifically 1 molecule of adenine), a ribose (oxolan ring), and 1 molecule of phosphate group, linked via the characteristic N-glycosidic bond and phosphate ester bond (additionally connected by a phosphodiester bond), bearing high stereochemical similarity to natural nucleotides like Guanosine Monophosphate (GMP); compared with ATP, it lacks 2 phosphate groups and has no high-energy phosphate bonds, making it one of the basic units of RNA. Nucleotides are the fundamental building blocks of all living organisms, participating in essential life processes such as energy storage (ATP), nucleic acid synthesis (DNA/RNA), and cell signaling (cAMP/cGMP), and for this compound specifically, functionally it mainly serves as a precursor for nucleotide synthesis: it can be phosphorylated to form ADP (adenosine diphosphate), which is further converted into ATP, participating in the substrate cycle of cellular energy metabolism, meanwhile it is an important component of RNA, involved in the transmission and expression of genetic information, and like ATP and adenosine, it can effectively elute specifically bound RNAs from ATP-agarose columns, demonstrating that its adenosine moiety (the core structure shared with ATP) is key for RNA binding, while the number of phosphate groups has little impact on this binding process; thus, if this compound is the natural nucleotide itself, it performs its endogenous physiological functions within the cell; if it is a synthetic analog, it would be designed as an inhibitor or substrate for purine metabolism enzymes or reverse transcriptase, primarily used for antiviral or anti-cancer therapy by being incorporated into nucleic acid chains to cause chain termination.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: (2R)-6-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]hexanamide, also referred to as 2H-K5NPr, is an ultra-large oligomer characterized by the attachment of the benzimidazole-piperazine-butyramide active unit to a long peptidic chain composed of four repeating alanine-amide units and a D-lysine derivative backbone, this compound showed poor potency (being the weakest among the lysine-based PTAs) with no strong cellular benefits reported, which highlighted the disadvantage of overly extended spacers and confirmed that optimal linker length lies at four units as longer chains compromise recognition, leading to it not being pursued further, while its oligomerization is intended to increase the molecules length and rigidity, enabling it to occupy a longer stretch of the DNA minor groove or bind to multiple DNA sites in a multivalent fashion, thereby seeking higher target specificity and cellular toxicity, and the benzimidazole group serves as the core DNA binding unit, while the lysine backbone increases the molecules cationicity and potential for tumor cell targeting, representing a highly sophisticated class of DNA-binding drugs, with potential activity as a high-affinity anti-cancer or anti-fungal agent via interference with nucleic acid function.

Small_molecule_information: (2R)-6-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]hexanamide, also referred to as 2H-K4NH, is a large, multivalent peptide-heterocycle conjugate with multiple chiral centers, which exhibited an IC comparable to K4NPr, demonstrated good cellular activity in splicing correction assays, but had lower proteolytic stability than K4NMe (a property that limits its potential as a lead scaffold) and highlighted that tertiary amides improve both stability and activity; the framework of this compound consists of an oligopeptide chain built from a (2R)-lysine and four (2R)-alanine residues, a key feature is its multivalency as the same complex bis-benzimidazole-butanoyl unit is attached at both the N-terminus and the -amino group of the lysine residue, the bis-benzimidazole unit is recognized as a potent DNA minor groove binder, this bivalent design strategy aims to dramatically enhance the binding affinity, specificity, and overall potency towards DNA through cooperative binding, and such a compound is a strong candidate for a highly effective DNA targeting agent in cancer therapy or as a high-sensitivity probe for nuclear staining and imaging.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: 5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 1f, is a pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole derivative (the 1a-derived bis-benzimidazole series member) with the tetracyclic aromatic heterocycle pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole as its parent structure: this compound lacks the hydroxyl group at position 9 but retains the methyl groups at positions 5 and 11 (while keeping the 9-hydroxy-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazolium core but being stripped of the cationic piperidinium side chain and carrying only a neutral 2-hydroxyethyl tail), making it a neutral tetracyclic aromatic molecule with the molecular formula C19H16N2, and this single structural change abolishes its high-affinity binding to the pathogenic 5CGG/3GGC internal loops of the r(CGG)exp RNA hairpin, eliminates detectable disruption of the toxic r(CGG)12DGCR8 complex, results in no rescue of SMN2 or Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing and no reduction in r(CGG)exp nuclear foci in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells at relevant concentrations, while its lack of measurable activity, low cytotoxicity and easy synthesis make it a useful negative-control probe for validating the absolute requirement of a permanent positive charge in next-generation ligands aimed at CGG-repeat disorders, and as a fundamental structure of ellipticine derivatives, it may retain the potential for DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition activity or serve as a precursor for more active derivatives.

Small_molecule_information: 2-ethyl-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-2-ium-9-ol, also referred to as Compound 1c (a bis-benzimidazole analog that retains the 9-hydroxy-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazolium core of 1a but carries a shortened 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl tail in place of the piperidinium side chain), is a pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole quaternary ammonium salt analog; this compound preserves high-affinity recognition of the 5CGG/3GGC internal loops within the r(CGG)exp hairpin and maintains relevant potency in disrupting the pathogenic r(CGG)12DGCR8 complex, can restore a large proportion of wild-type SMN2 and Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells expressing relevant CGG repeats while halving both the number and nuclear volume of r(CGG)exp-containing foci without altering Sam68-independent transcripts or gross cellular viability, and has favorable aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity and efficient synthesis which make it a validated chemical probe for dissecting r(CGG)exp-mediated toxicity and an attractive alternate lead scaffold for developing therapies against FXTAS and related CGG-repeat disorders, while due to its known DNA intercalation properties, it is also researched as a potential anticancer drug and Topoisomerase II inhibitor.

RNA information: RNA 2 (Drosha 515) is an RNA fragment that mimics the Drosha processing site in pri-miR-515, with its core structural feature being the presence of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515. Functionally, it serves as an in vitro research tool for detecting the binding properties between small molecules and RNA: the monomeric Compound 1 can bind to it, while the dimeric Compound 2 has no binding ability to it. Through such interaction analyses, it provides key data support for screening selective small molecules that target pri-miR-515.

N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, featuring multiple repeating amide units and a benzimidazole core with an extremely complex molecular formula, and it is a dimeric small molecule designed via fragment assembly that features a peptidomimetic scaffold linking two RNA-binding modules of Compound 1 through four propylamine spacers, adopts an intramolecular structure, does not bind to DNA or tRNA, selectively binds to the Drosha processing site and adjacent motif of pri-miR-515 to inhibit its processing into mature miRNA, which in turn upregulates SK1 and HER2 expression, induces cancer cell apoptosis, sensitizes HER2 cancer cells to Herceptin, has no effect on normal cells and provides tools and insights for RNA-targeted anticancer drug development, this compound presents as a large molecule with high lipophilicity and multiple coordination sites, functionally, this type of compound is likely used as a macromolecular ligand, potentially for targeting specific intracellular signaling pathways or serving as a prodrug for drug delivery in areas like oncology, and also plays a role in RNA-targeted anticancer drug development with its unique mechanism of action on pri-miR-515 and related cancer cell regulation.

RNA information: NA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.; RNA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1 (Targaprimir-515/885), is a complex molecular probe based on the bis-benzimidazole (DNA minor groove binder) scaffold and modified with an azide group (-N) and bulky tert-butyl groups, and it is also an azide-containing monomeric small molecule that serves as an RNA-binding module coupling to peptidomimetic scaffolds via Cu-catalyzed azide-alkyne click chemistry, which binds similarly to Drosha processing site motifs of pri-miR-515 and pri-miR-885 plus an adjacent motif of pri-miR-515 to inhibit both miRNAs biogenesis and acts as a lead for selective dimers like Compound 2 to aid RNA-targeting small molecule optimization; in chemical biology, the azide group is the key functional group for Click Chemistry, its real-world activity is designed as a chemically taggable DNA-binding probe, and it is used to study the biological targets of the bis-benzimidazole core by attaching a reporter molecule (e.g., fluorophore or biotin) to the binding site via a highly specific click reaction.

RNA information: pre-miRNA-210, the precursor of miR-210, has a hairpin structure and contains a Dicer processing site (5' ACU 3'/3' UCA 5'), which can be cleaved by Dicer enzyme to generate mature miR-210. Its core function is regulating hypoxia-related pathways: mature miR-210 inhibits GPD1L expression, impairs PHD activity, preventing HIF-1 from degradation and activating downstream pro-cancer transcriptional responses. It is significantly upregulated under hypoxic conditions and closely associated with tumor metastasis.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Targapremir-210 (a bis-benzimidazole small molecule from which derivatives containing chlorambucil cross-linking module and biotin purification module can be synthesized), is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe (without tert-butyl groups); the subtle difference in the linkers attachment point (meta-position) is an important factor in structure-activity relationship (SAR) studies, and functionally it binds to the Dicer processing site of pre-miR-210, inhibiting its processing into mature miR-210, upregulates GPD1L, downregulates HIF-1, induces apoptosis of hypoxic breast cancer cells, inhibits tumor growth, has good selectivity and tumor localization ability, while its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe used to investigate how minor structural changes influence the molecules DNA binding affinity and selectivity.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-7-[(2R,4S,5R,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-9-(2-hydroxyacetyl)-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione, also referred to as Epirubicin, is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Epirubicin, which is an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent that intercalates into DNA, inhibiting topoisomerase II and causing DNA strand breaks; Epirubicin is a widely used chemotherapy drug in clinical practice for treating various cancers such as breast cancer and ovarian cancer, and its mechanism involves DNA intercalation and Topoisomerase II inhibition.

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(trifluoromethyl)-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as G1, is a purine derivative where the core structure is a purine, which forms the basis of nucleic acids like adenine and guanine, and it is also a hydrophobic derivative of trifluoromethyl (-CF) introduced at the C2 position of guanine that can induce RNA to form a compact structure similar to guanine; this compound features a strong electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl (CF) group at the 2-position, significantly altering the electronic distribution, acid-base properties, and lipophilicity of the purine ring, and although it can bind to ribose switches, it has no significant inhibitory effect on the growth of Bacillus subtilis, suggesting it lacks antibacterial activity, while purine analogues are vital in biology and are often explored as antiviral, anticancer agents, or enzyme inhibitors by mimicking endogenous purine nucleosides to interfere with metabolic processes, and the trifluoromethyl substituent is typically used to enhance the molecules metabolic stability or modify its binding affinity to specific targets, such as kinases or purine metabolizing enzymes, making it potentially useful as a chemical probe or drug lead candidate for studying purine metabolism.
